title: 11 1 Partial Differential Equations
body:
TOPIC 11: PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS I 11.1 PDES AND D‚ÄôALEMBERT‚ÄôS SOLUTION

The equation ‚ąā 2u 2 2‚ąā u = c (1) ‚ąāt2 ‚ąāx2 is called the one-dimensional wave equation.

It models the oscillations of a tightly stretched string.

The solution u(x, t) describes the displacement of the string at position x and time t.

The constant c is determined by the physical characteristics of the string.

The general solution to the one dimensional wave equation is

u = ŌÜ(x + ct) + Ōą(x ‚ąí ct) (2)

where ŌÜ and Ōą are arbitrary functions. (This is D‚ÄôAlembert‚Äôs solution of the wave equation)

If we have an initial displacement of

u(x, 0) = f (x)

then the solution to the one dimensional wave equation is u(x, t) = [f (x + ct) + f (x ‚ąí ct)]

If we have an initial displacement of

‚ąāu(x, 0) u(x, 0) = f (x) and an initial velocity of = g(x) ‚ąāt then the solution to the one dimensional wave equation is

1 x+ct Z u(x, t) = [f (x + ct) + f (x ‚ąí ct)] + g(s)ds. 2 2c x‚ąíct

Basic concepts An equation involving one or more partial derivatives of an (unknown) function of two or more independent variables is called a partial differential equation.

‚ÄĘ The order of the highest derivative is called the order of the equation.

‚ÄĘ A p.d.e (partial differential equation) is linear if it is linear in all terms involving u and its partial derivatives

‚ÄĘ A solution of a p.d.e. is a function of several variables which satisfies the equation.

Some examples

‚ąā 2u 2 2‚ąā u (1) = c one dimensional wave equation ‚ąāt2 ‚ąāx2 ‚ąāu ‚ąā 2u (2) = c2 2 one dimensional heat equation ‚ąāt ‚ąāx ‚ąā 2u ‚ąā 2u (3) + =0 two dimensional Laplace equation ‚ąāx2 ‚ąāy 2 ‚ąāu 2 ‚ąāu (4) ( ) = ‚ąāt ‚ąāx

(1)-(3) are second order, linear. (4) is first order non-linear.

We may, depending on the problem, have boundary conditions (the solution has some given value on the boundary of some domain) or initial conditions (where the value of the solution will be given at some initial time, e.g. t = 0).

There are few general methods for solving p.d.e‚Äôs. Sometimes simple partial integration will work and we look at some elementary techniques in this lecture. However the main tool is a process called separation of variable which (together with the associated use of Fourier series) will be examined in detail for the remainder of the course.

Example 1 Solve the partial differential equation

‚ąā 2u = cos(y). ‚ąāx‚ąāy

F u(x, t) = x sin(y) + ŌÜ(x) + Ōą(y) F

We see from the above example that there is a lot of freedom (probably too much) in the solution of a p.d.e. This is why boundary and initial conditions play such a central role.

Example 2 Verify that u(x, t) = e‚ąít sin(3x) is a solution to the one dimensional heat equation ‚ąāu 1 ‚ąā 2u = ‚ąāt 9 ‚ąāx2

Example 3 Consider the one dimensional wave equation

‚ąā 2u ‚ąā 2u = 25 ‚ąāt2 ‚ąāx2 with initial displacment

‚ąāu(x, 0) u(x, 0) = 6ex and an initial velocity of = 10 cos(x) ‚ąāt Show that D‚ÄôAlembert‚Äôs solution is

u(x, t) = 3ex+5t + 3ex‚ąí5t + sin(x + 5t) ‚ąí sin(x ‚ąí 5t).

Verify that both the p.d.e. and the initial conditions are satisfied.

The full proof for D‚ÄôAlembert‚Äôs solution (making extensive use of the chain rule) is in your printed notes.

Unfortunately D‚ÄôAlembert‚Äôs solution only works for the wave equation. Separation of variables is a more general technique which will keep us busy for the remainder of the course. We will apply separation of variables not only to the wave equation but also to a host of other p.d.e‚Äôs. You can now do Q 114 115
